Adversarially review implemented task T119 ROUND 3 (goal G7, defect D36). Return the structured verdict JSON {taskId, verdict:"approve"|"disapprove", criticism:[], questions:[], defects:[], rationale, summary} and WRITE NOTHING to the ledger.

Fetch acceptance: `mcp__ledger__fetch_item` ledger=tasks item=T119.
Branch implement/T119-r3 at commit ff18ccd036c34e98783f75b50b7629e7148ce629, based at main 36f0bd3 (worker reset from a stale base; VERIFY `git merge-base 36f0bd3 ff18ccd` == 36f0bd3 and review merge-base..HEAD). Worktree: /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-ab106078a231db918.
Diff: `git -C /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia diff 36f0bd3 ff18ccd036c34e98783f75b50b7629e7148ce629`.

CONTEXT: This is the HARDEST task of the run — three prior review rounds each found a distinct permanent-blackhole in the resequencer restart logic (all empirically reproduced). Rounds 1+2 fixes are verified. Round 3 adds:
- FIX 3 (bound the gate): a pendingLowDrops counter; after O(window) consecutive pendingLow suspect-drops, admit falls back to a plain unpin (started=false) that self-heals — so a lost seq-1 init frame at anchor==window+2 no longer permanently blackholes.
- FIX 4a (ring clear): the admit low re-anchor branch now clears the ring/buf/waiting (mirroring resync), so no stale old-boot cell survives to jump next high or corrupt buf.
- FIX 4b (gate FEC): ObserveRecovered drops recovered frames while pendingLow is armed.
- Counter reset wired into RebaselineToLow (arm) and Rebaseline (clear).

Be MAXIMALLY adversarial — the last two rounds each looked green but hid a blackhole. Verify: (1) FIX 3 — the drop-counter fallback genuinely PREVENTS permanent blackhole at EVERY armed boundary (anchor==window+2 with seq-1 lost now RECOVERS; check the counter threshold, that it counts only CONSECUTIVE pendingLow suspect-drops, resets correctly, and that a legitimate slow-arriving low init isn't prematurely abandoned into a wrong anchor); (2) FIX 4a — the ring/buf/waiting clear is COMPLETE (no accounting left inconsistent) and matches resync's invariants; (3) FIX 4b — ObserveRecovered-while-armed drops cleanly (no buf leak, no lost legitimate post-restart recovery); (4) NONE of the round-1/round-2 fixes, the saturated D36 case, stale-high race, per-peer scoping, lock discipline, or D32 tests regressed; (5) the 4 new tests genuinely discriminate (the 2 new-fix tests fail on round-2 source). Try to CONSTRUCT a new permanent-blackhole or stale-delivery scenario the fixes miss (e.g. counter never reaching threshold under a specific interleaving, a partial ring-clear, ObserveRecovered after the gate clears but before re-anchor). Run `cd /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-ab106078a231db918 && nix develop -c sh -c 'go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./internal/bind/... ./internal/reseq/...' && nix develop -c sh -c 'go test -race ./internal/bind/...' && nix develop -c just lint`. Split criticism[] vs questions[]; file genuine defects.

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.

- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).

diff --git a/internal/reseq/reseq.go b/internal/reseq/reseq.go
index 62fe1a1..0dd7cc3 100644
--- a/internal/reseq/reseq.go
+++ b/internal/reseq/reseq.go
@@ -144,6 +144,26 @@ type Resequencer struct {
 	resyncLo   uint64
 	resyncHi   uint64
 
+	// Pending low-anchor re-baseline (peer restart, T119). After RebaselineToLow the
+	// release point is UNPINNED but NOT re-anchored on the next arbitrary frame:
+	// pendingLow keeps `next` held at pendingLowAnchor (the pre-rebaseline release
+	// point) so a stale HIGH-seq straggler still draining from the OLD boot is
+	// SUSPECT-dropped, and `next` re-anchors ONLY when a genuine restarted-stream
+	// low-seq — a frame more than one window BELOW pendingLowAnchor — arrives. This
+	// closes the D36 re-pin race the plain Rebaseline leaves open (plan review R126).
+	pendingLow       bool
+	pendingLowAnchor uint64
+
+	// pendingLowDrops counts consecutive SUSPECT drops taken while the low-anchor gate
+	// is armed. It BOUNDS the gate so it can never permanently blackhole (round-3 FIX 3,
+	// review R150): at anchor == window+2 the only in-budget re-anchor frame is seq 1, so
+	// if that lone wrapped-init frame is LOST every later new-boot frame fails
+	// `anchor - seq > window` and would be SUSPECT-dropped forever. After O(window) such
+	// drops the gate FALLS BACK to a plain unpin (started=false), which self-heals via the
+	// existing resync-corroboration path. Reset to 0 whenever the gate is (re-)armed or
+	// cleared.
+	pendingLowDrops uint64
+
 	// Diagnostics (read via the accessors; useful for the bounded-memory asserts).
 	highWater   int    // max occupied slots ever held
 	dropDup     uint64 // frames dropped as duplicates
@@ -233,6 +253,21 @@ func (r *Resequencer) ObserveRecovered(seq uint64, payload []byte, src netip.Add
 	r.mu.Lock()
 	defer r.mu.Unlock()
 
+	if r.pendingLow {
+		// A low-anchor re-baseline (peer restart) is armed: a parity-RECOVERED frame is
+		// by definition part of the PRE-restart stream — the gate is up precisely because
+		// a restart re-anchor is pending, and a frame recoverable from the old boot's
+		// parity belongs to that old boot. Seating it would place a stale old-boot cell in
+		// [anchor, anchor+window) that keeps the head-of-line timeout live and lets
+		// expire() jump `next` high past the restarted stream (round-3 FIX 4b, review
+		// R150). ObserveRecovered otherwise bypasses admit entirely, so without this it
+		// never consults the gate. Drop it — FEC recovery must not seat state while the
+		// gate arbitrates the restart. Returns false so the /metrics recovered counter
+		// stays honest (nothing was delivered).
+		r.dropSuspect++
+		return false
+	}
+
 	if !r.started {
 		r.started = true
 		r.next = seq

1	package reseq
2	
3	import (
4		"net/netip"
5		"sync"
6		"time"
7	)
8	
9	// Clock is the injected time source. Production wires a monotonic wall clock
10	// (SystemClock); tests wire a hand-advanced fake so ordering, window, and
11	// timeout behaviour are deterministic with no real sleeps. It is intentionally
12	// structurally identical to telemetry.Clock so the same fake can drive both.
13	type Clock interface {
14		Now() time.Time
15	}
16	
17	// SystemClock is the production Clock backed by the monotonic wall clock.
18	type SystemClock struct{}
19	
20	// Now returns the current monotonic time.
21	func (SystemClock) Now() time.Time { return time.Now() }
22	
23	// Discontinuity/resync guard tuning. A DATA frame is UNAUTHENTICATED by design
24	// (internal/frame/frame.go: "DATA/PARITY forgeable by design"), so a garbage
25	// datagram decoding as KindData (p ~ 1/256) delivers a uniformly-random uint64
26	// OuterSeq, and a genuine peer PROCESS RESTART resets the peer's outerSeq counter
27	// to 1. Neither may be trusted to move the release point arbitrarily.
28	const (
29		// resyncFactor (K) bounds how far AHEAD of the release point a single
30		// unauthenticated frame is trusted to advance the window as a plausible loss
31		// burst. Under bounded cross-path reorder a legitimate frame sits within one
32		// window of next; a forward jump up to K windows is still treated as a loss
33		// burst (the sound bounded-memory window-advance handles it in O(window)), but
34		// a jump of K windows or more is SUSPECT: a single such frame never advances
35		// next. K=4 leaves generous headroom above the 1-window reorder bound while
36		// capping the release point a single trusted frame can skip to (K-1) windows.
37		resyncFactor = 4
38	
39		// resyncCorroborate (C) is how many out-of-band frames carrying DISTINCT seqs
40		// that mutually span less than one window must be observed before the release
41		// point is re-pinned. A genuine peer restart (1,2,3,...) or a consistent
42		// long-outage forward jump emits connected, distinct seqs that trivially
43		// corroborate. A repeated identical seq does NOT advance the count: a single
44		// junk or forged datagram re-delivered — a network duplicate, or a forger
45		// replaying one datagram — contributes only ONE distinct seq, so it can never
46		// self-corroborate. Corroboration therefore requires C INDEPENDENT junk seqs to
47		// land mutually within one window; each is independent in 2^64, so that occurs
48		// with probability ~(window/2^64)^(C-1) ~ 1e-32 for C=3 and window=2048 — junk
49		// never triggers a resync, while a real discontinuity resyncs after losing at
50		// most C-1 frames. Any in-band (near-current) frame resets the run, so ordinary
51		// traffic interspersed with rare junk never accumulates a false corroboration.
52		resyncCorroborate = 3
53	)
54	
55	// Item is one released inner datagram plus the outer source address of the DATA
56	// frame that carried it. The source travels with the payload so the multipath
57	// Bind can pin its single virtual endpoint to the FIRST delivered frame's
58	// source even when that frame was buffered and released out of arrival order.
59	type Item struct {
60		Payload []byte
61		Src     netip.AddrPort
62	}
63	
64	// slot is one ring cell. A live cell (occupied) holds exactly one buffered DATA
65	// frame whose OuterSeq maps to this index; seq is retained so a stale cell (an
66	// already-drained seq that shares the index modulo window) is never mistaken for
67	// a live one.
68	type slot struct {
69		seq      uint64
70		src      netip.AddrPort
71		payload  []byte
72		occupied bool
73	}
74	
75	// Resequencer is a bounded-window, timeout-based receive resequencing buffer. It
76	// consumes DATA frames tagged with the multipath codec's OWN outer sequence
77	// number (frame.Data.OuterSeq — never WireGuard's inner counter) arriving out of
78	// order across paths, and releases the decoded inner datagrams in strictly
79	// ascending outer-seq order before they reach the WireGuard engine. This absorbs
80	// multipath reorder entirely in the outer layer so WG's RFC 6479 anti-replay
81	// filter (window 8128, see docs/p0-findings.md §6) only ever sees monotonically
82	// increasing inner counters and never drops a legitimately-delivered packet.
83	//
84	// Guarantees (see the package tests):
85	//   - Ordering: released frames are strictly ascending in outer-seq. A frame
86	//     whose seq is below the release point is dropped, never delivered late, so
87	//     delivery is monotonic and WG sees no replay-window regression.
88	//   - Exactly-once: duplicates (same seq re-observed before or after release) are
89	//     dropped; every deliverable frame is released exactly once.
90	//   - Bounded memory: at most `window` frames are buffered. A frame at or beyond
91	//     next+window forces the window to advance (releasing/skipping the tail),
92	//     so an adversarial reorder/loss trace cannot grow the buffer without bound.
93	//   - Timeout progress: when the head-of-line seq is missing, buffered frames
94	//     ahead of the gap are held at most `timeout`; then the gap is skipped
95	//     (treated as lost) and the run released, rather than stalling forever. Each
96	//     distinct head-of-line gap gets its OWN full timeout.
97	//   - Bounded compute: every Observe runs in O(window). No frame — however wild
98	//     its (unauthenticated, possibly forged) seq — can make the release point
99	//     advance one seq at a time across a gap of up to 2^64 under the mutex.
100	//   - Discontinuity resilience: an unauthenticated frame whose seq lies farther
101	//     than resyncFactor*window from next never moves the release point on its own.
102	//     A genuine peer restart or long outage — a run of frames carrying DISTINCT
103	//     seqs that mutually fall within one window — re-pins (resyncs) the release
104	//     point within a bounded number of frames; uniformly-random junk seqs, and a
105	//     single junk/forged seq re-delivered any number of times, do not.
106	//
107	// Concurrency: every method is guarded by one mutex, so the shared instance is
108	// safe for the multipath Bind's per-path receive goroutines. The mutex is held
109	// only for in-memory bookkeeping — never across a syscall — so it does not
110	// perturb the Bind's lock-free virtual-endpoint fast path or its
111	// syscall-outside-mutex send discipline.
112	//
113	// FEC seam (T24): Observe is the sole ingestion point and is keyed purely by
114	// outer-seq. An FEC decoder inserted on the receive path recovers missing DATA
115	// frames from PARITY and feeds each recovered frame's ORIGINAL outer-seq through
116	// Observe, identically to a natively-received frame — so FEC recovery slots in
117	// BEFORE resequencing with no change to this type.
118	type Resequencer struct {
119		window  uint64
120		timeout time.Duration
121		clock   Clock
122	
123		mu      sync.Mutex
124		started bool   // false until the first Observe pins the initial next
125		next    uint64 // lowest outer-seq not yet released
126		ring    []slot // len == window; indexed by seq % window
127		buf     int    // number of occupied slots
128	
129		ready    []Item // FIFO of released items awaiting Pop
130		readyPos int    // read cursor into ready (front of the FIFO)
131	
132		// Head-of-line timeout: when next is a gap but frames ahead are buffered,
133		// waiting is armed with a deadline measured from when the gap first formed.
134		waiting  bool
135		deadline time.Time
136	
137		// Discontinuity/resync guard: the current run of out-of-band (suspect) frames.
138		// resyncSeqs holds the DISTINCT suspect seqs seen in the run (a repeated
139		// identical seq does not advance corroboration); [resyncLo, resyncHi] is the
140		// run's seq span. When resyncSeqs reaches resyncCorroborate distinct seqs the
141		// run is deemed a real discontinuity and the release point re-pins (see
142		// tryResync). It holds at most resyncCorroborate seqs, so memory stays bounded.
143		resyncSeqs []uint64
144		resyncLo   uint64
145		resyncHi   uint64
146	
147		// Diagnostics (read via the accessors; useful for the bounded-memory asserts).
148		highWater   int    // max occupied slots ever held
149		dropDup     uint64 // frames dropped as duplicates
150		dropLate    uint64 // frames dropped as already-past the release point
151		dropSuspect uint64 // out-of-band frames dropped while (not yet) corroborating
152		skipped     uint64 // seqs skipped (treated lost) by window-advance or timeout
153		releasedN   uint64 // frames released for delivery
154		resyncs     uint64 // release-point re-pins after a corroborated discontinuity
155		rebaselines uint64 // release-point re-baselines forced by a trusted control event (hub failover)
156	}
157	
158	// New returns a resequencer buffering at most window outer-seq positions and
159	// holding a head-of-line-blocked run for at most timeout before skipping the gap.
160	// window must be positive; timeout may be zero (a missing head seq is skipped as
161	// soon as the next Observe/Pop advances the clock past the arrival instant).
162	func New(window uint64, timeout time.Duration, clock Clock) *Resequencer {
163		if window == 0 {
164			panic("reseq: window must be positive")
165		}
166		if clock == nil {
167			panic("reseq: clock must be non-nil")
168		}
169		return &Resequencer{
170			window:  window,
171			timeout: timeout,
172			clock:   clock,
173			ring:    make([]slot, window),
174		}
175	}
176	
177	// Observe ingests one DATA frame's inner payload under its outer-seq. It takes
178	// ownership of payload (the caller must not mutate it afterwards); the multipath
179	// Bind passes the freshly-decoded frame.Data.Payload, which aliases nothing else.
180	// src is the outer source address the frame arrived from. Any frames that become
181	// deliverable are appended to the ready FIFO for Pop.
182	func (r *Resequencer) Observe(seq uint64, payload []byte, src netip.AddrPort) {
183		now := r.clock.Now()
184		r.mu.Lock()
185		defer r.mu.Unlock()
186	
187		if !r.started {
188			r.started = true
189			r.next = seq
190		}
191	
192		// Give up on any head-of-line gap whose hold time has elapsed before deciding
193		// where this frame lands.
194		r.expire(now)
195	
196		// Classify the frame against the release window and the discontinuity guard.
197		// admit adjusts next (window-advance or resync) when warranted and reports
198		// whether the frame now lands inside [next, next+window) and should be placed.
199		if !r.admit(seq) {
200			return
201		}
202	
203		cell := &r.ring[seq%r.window]
204		if cell.occupied && cell.seq == seq {
205			r.dropDup++
206			return
207		}
208		cell.seq = seq
209		cell.src = src
210		cell.payload = payload
211		cell.occupied = true
212		r.buf++
213		if r.buf > r.highWater {
214			r.highWater = r.buf
215		}
216	
217		r.drain()
218		r.arm(now)
219	}
220	
221	// ObserveRecovered ingests a payload the FEC decoder reconstructed from parity (T24).
222	// Unlike Observe it NEVER moves or re-pins the release point: a recovered frame is a
223	// REPAIR for a past gap, so if it has already fallen below the release point it is
224	// dropped as too-late, and it can neither window-advance nor corroborate a resync — so
225	// FEC recovery can never CAUSE the burst loss it exists to prevent (a late batch of K
226	// recovered seqs below `next` must not be able to dump the live buffer via a backward
227	// resync). It returns whether the frame was PLACED (landed at or above the release
228	// point and will be delivered in order); a dropped-late recovered frame returns false,
229	// which the datapath uses to keep the /metrics recovered counter honest — counting
230	// frames actually delivered ahead of the release point, not merely reconstructed.
231	func (r *Resequencer) ObserveRecovered(seq uint64, payload []byte, src netip.AddrPort) bool {
232		now := r.clock.Now()
233		r.mu.Lock()
234		defer r.mu.Unlock()
235	
236		if !r.started {
237			r.started = true
238			r.next = seq
239		}
240		r.expire(now)
241	
242		// The placement window is EXACTLY [next, next+window). A recovered frame below next
243		// is too late (drop, and — crucially — never feed the resync guard). One at or beyond
244		// next+window would require a window-advance we refuse to perform for a reconstructed
245		// frame, since that would skip live seqs on the strength of a repair. Neither branch
246		// touches next or the corroboration run.
247		if seq < r.next {
248			if r.next-seq > r.window {
249				r.dropSuspect++
250			} else {
251				r.dropLate++
252			}
253			return false
254		}
255		if seq-r.next >= r.window {
256			r.dropLate++
257			return false
258		}
259	
260		cell := &r.ring[seq%r.window]
261		if cell.occupied && cell.seq == seq {
262			r.dropDup++
263			return false // already buffered (a received frame or a prior recovery)
264		}
265		cell.seq = seq
266		cell.src = src
267		cell.payload = payload
268		cell.occupied = true
269		r.buf++
270		if r.buf > r.highWater {
271			r.highWater = r.buf
272		}
273	
274		r.drain()
275		r.arm(now)
276		return true
277	}
278	
279	// admit classifies seq against the release window and the discontinuity guard and
280	// decides whether the frame should be buffered. It returns true when seq now lies
281	// inside [next, next+window) and should be placed; false (having bumped the
282	// appropriate drop counter) when the frame is dropped. It may advance next by a
283	// bounded amount on a plausible loss burst, or re-pin next on a corroborated
284	// discontinuity. Caller holds r.mu.
285	func (r *Resequencer) admit(seq uint64) bool {
286		if seq < r.next {
287			// Below the release point. A frame more than one window below next is
288			// impossibly late under bounded reorder — treat it as SUSPECT (a peer
289			// restart resets outerSeq to 1, so its frames land here). A frame within a
290			// window below next is an ordinary straggler/duplicate.
291			if r.next-seq > r.window {
292				if r.tryResync(seq) {
293					return true
294				}
295				r.dropSuspect++
296				return false
297			}
298			r.resyncReset() // near-current traffic: not a discontinuity
299			r.dropLate++
300			return false
301		}
302		// seq >= next.
303		if seq-r.next >= resyncFactor*r.window {
304			// Too far ahead to be a plausible loss burst — SUSPECT (garbage-decoded or
305			// forged high seq). A single such frame must not advance next.
306			if r.tryResync(seq) {
307				return true
308			}
309			r.dropSuspect++
310			return false
311		}
312		// In-window, or a moderate (plausible loss-burst) forward jump: legitimate
313		// near-current traffic, so any in-progress discontinuity run is broken.
314		r.resyncReset()
315		if seq-r.next >= r.window {
316			// Beyond the window: advance the release point so the frame fits at the top
317			// of the window, releasing buffered frames below the new base in order and
318			// skipping the (assumed lost) gaps. This is what bounds memory.
319			r.advanceTo(seq - r.window + 1)
320		}
321		return true
322	}
323	
324	// Pop returns the next in-order released inner datagram, or ok=false when none is
325	// ready. It also advances the head-of-line timeout, so a receive goroutine that
326	// only drains still makes timeout progress.
327	func (r *Resequencer) Pop() (Item, bool) {
328		now := r.clock.Now()
329		r.mu.Lock()
330		defer r.mu.Unlock()
331	
332		r.expire(now)
333	
334		if r.readyPos >= len(r.ready) {
335			return Item{}, false
336		}
337		it := r.ready[r.readyPos]
338		r.ready[r.readyPos] = Item{} // release the payload reference
339		r.readyPos++
340		if r.readyPos >= len(r.ready) {
341			// FIFO drained: reset to reuse the backing array, keeping it bounded.
342			r.ready = r.ready[:0]
343			r.readyPos = 0
344		}
345		return it, true
346	}
347	
348	// drain releases the contiguous run starting at next into the ready FIFO. Caller
349	// holds r.mu.
350	func (r *Resequencer) drain() {
351		for {
352			cell := &r.ring[r.next%r.window]
353			if !cell.occupied || cell.seq != r.next {
354				return
355			}
356			r.release(cell)
357			r.next++
358		}
359	}
360	
361	// advanceTo moves next up to target, releasing any occupied cell it passes (in
362	// ascending order) and counting the empty positions as skipped (lost) seqs. It is
363	// O(window), NEVER O(target-next): all occupied cells live in [next, next+window),
364	// so once next reaches next+window the remaining gap is provably empty and is
365	// closed by arithmetic rather than iterated. Caller holds r.mu; target must be
366	// > next.
367	func (r *Resequencer) advanceTo(target uint64) {
368		// Only [next, next+window) can hold occupied cells; iterate at most that far.
369		limit := target
370		if limit > r.next+r.window {
371			limit = r.next + r.window
372		}
373		for r.next < limit {
374			cell := &r.ring[r.next%r.window]
375			if cell.occupied && cell.seq == r.next {
376				r.release(cell)
377			} else {
378				r.skipped++
379			}
380			r.next++
381		}
382		if r.next < target {
383			// The remaining gap [next, target) is entirely empty — no occupied cell can
384			// live a full window ahead of the old release point. Close it by arithmetic.
385			r.skipped += target - r.next
386			r.next = target
387		}
388	}
389	
390	// release moves one occupied cell's payload into the ready FIFO and clears it.
391	// Caller holds r.mu.
392	func (r *Resequencer) release(cell *slot) {
393		r.ready = append(r.ready, Item{Payload: cell.payload, Src: cell.src})
394		cell.occupied = false
395		cell.payload = nil
396		r.buf--
397		r.releasedN++
398	}
399	
400	// expire skips a head-of-line gap whose hold time has elapsed: it jumps next to
401	// the smallest buffered seq (treating the intervening seqs as lost) and releases
402	// the now-contiguous run. Caller holds r.mu.
403	func (r *Resequencer) expire(now time.Time) {
404		if !r.waiting || now.Before(r.deadline) {
405			return
406		}
407		minSeq, ok := r.smallestBuffered()
408		if !ok {
409			r.waiting = false
410			return
411		}
412		// Skip the lost gap [next, minSeq), then release from minSeq onward.
413		for r.next < minSeq {
414			r.skipped++
415			r.next++
416		}
417		r.drain()
418		// Clear the armed state BEFORE re-arming so that a distinct SECOND gap exposed
419		// by this release gets its own full timeout rather than inheriting this gap's
420		// already-elapsed deadline (which would skip it with ~zero hold).
421		r.waiting = false
422		r.arm(now)
423	}
424	
425	// arm (re)evaluates the head-of-line timeout after a change. When next is a gap
426	// but buffered frames sit ahead of it, it starts the hold clock (only if not
427	// already running, so the deadline measures from when the gap first formed).
428	// Otherwise it disarms. Caller holds r.mu.
429	func (r *Resequencer) arm(now time.Time) {
430		cell := &r.ring[r.next%r.window]
431		headPresent := cell.occupied && cell.seq == r.next
432		if !headPresent && r.buf > 0 {
433			if !r.waiting {
434				r.waiting = true
435				r.deadline = now.Add(r.timeout)
436			}
437		} else {
438			r.waiting = false
439		}
440	}
441	
442	// tryResync feeds one out-of-band (suspect) seq into the discontinuity guard. It
443	// re-pins the release point ONLY after resyncCorroborate DISTINCT suspect seqs
444	// whose values mutually span less than one window (a genuine peer restart or long
445	// outage emits connected, distinct seqs that corroborate; uniformly-random junk
446	// seqs, each independent in 2^64, do not, and a single junk/forged seq re-delivered
447	// contributes only ONE distinct value so it can never self-corroborate). It
448	// returns true and performs the resync when the corroboration threshold is met —
449	// re-pinning next to the triggering seq so the caller places it as the new head
450	// and delivery resumes immediately, without a phantom gap or an extra timeout —
451	// and false while still collecting or when the seq fails to corroborate the
452	// current run. Caller holds r.mu.
453	func (r *Resequencer) tryResync(seq uint64) bool {
454		if len(r.resyncSeqs) == 0 || r.spanExceeds(seq) {
455			// Start (or restart) the run at this seq: it does not corroborate the
456			// current run, so it becomes the anchor of a fresh one.
457			r.resyncLo, r.resyncHi = seq, seq
458			r.resyncSeqs = append(r.resyncSeqs[:0], seq)
459			return false
460		}
461		if r.runContains(seq) {
462			// A repeated identical seq does NOT advance corroboration: a lone junk or
463			// forged datagram re-delivered must not count as independent corroboration.
464			return false
465		}
466		if seq < r.resyncLo {
467			r.resyncLo = seq
468		}
469		if seq > r.resyncHi {
470			r.resyncHi = seq
471		}
472		r.resyncSeqs = append(r.resyncSeqs, seq)
473		if len(r.resyncSeqs) < resyncCorroborate {
474			return false
475		}
476		// Corroborated discontinuity: re-pin to the triggering seq and resume delivery.
477		r.resync(seq)
478		return true
479	}
480	
481	// runContains reports whether seq is already one of the distinct suspect seqs in
482	// the current corroboration run. The run holds at most resyncCorroborate seqs, so
483	// this scan is O(C). Caller holds r.mu.
484	func (r *Resequencer) runContains(seq uint64) bool {
485		for _, s := range r.resyncSeqs {
486			if s == seq {
487				return true
488			}
489		}
490		return false
491	}
492	
493	// spanExceeds reports whether adding seq to the current corroboration run would
494	// make its seq span reach or exceed one window (i.e. seq does not mutually fall
495	// within one window of the run so far). Caller holds r.mu.
496	func (r *Resequencer) spanExceeds(seq uint64) bool {
497		lo, hi := r.resyncLo, r.resyncHi
498		if seq < lo {
499			lo = seq
500		}
501		if seq > hi {
502			hi = seq
503		}
504		return hi-lo >= r.window
505	}
506	
507	// resync re-pins the release point to base after a confirmed discontinuity,
508	// discarding all buffered frames (they belong to the pre-discontinuity stream) in
509	// O(window). The already-released FIFO is untouched — those were legitimate prior
510	// deliveries. Caller holds r.mu.
511	func (r *Resequencer) resync(base uint64) {
512		for i := range r.ring {
513			r.ring[i] = slot{}
514		}
515		r.buf = 0
516		r.next = base
517		r.waiting = false
518		r.resyncReset()
519		r.resyncs++
520	}
521	
522	// resyncReset abandons the current corroboration run. Caller holds r.mu.
523	func (r *Resequencer) resyncReset() {
524		r.resyncSeqs = r.resyncSeqs[:0]
525		r.resyncLo = 0
526		r.resyncHi = 0
527	}
528	
529	// Rebaseline discards the buffered (pre-switch) frames and UNPINS the release
530	// point so the NEXT Observe re-anchors `next` to that frame's outer-seq, exactly as
531	// if the stream had just started. It exists for the edge-side HUB FAILOVER (T57): a
532	// concentrator switch changes the DATA-frame SENDER, and the standby is a separate
533	// process whose outer-seq restarts near 1 — far below the release point the prior
534	// hub's high-rate stream advanced `next` to. The unauthenticated tryResync path
535	// cannot rescue this in time: it needs resyncCorroborate (3) distinct low seqs
536	// within one window, but a freshly re-handshaking standby emits only ~1 DATA frame
537	// (the handshake response) per RekeyTimeout, so corroboration falls outside the
538	// failover window and the response is dropped as SUSPECT — the tunnel never
539	// re-establishes (defect D32). Unlike a DATA frame (forgeable by design, hence the
540	// corroboration guard), THIS re-pin is driven by a TRUSTED control event — the
541	// operator-configured ordered-endpoint switch, above the unauthenticated wire — so
542	// re-anchoring on the next single frame is sound. Buffered frames (from the dead
543	// prior hub) are discarded in O(window); the already-released FIFO of prior
544	// legitimate deliveries is untouched. Idempotent and safe before the first Observe
545	// (started stays false). Takes r.mu; the caller must NOT hold it.
546	func (r *Resequencer) Rebaseline() {
547		r.mu.Lock()
548		defer r.mu.Unlock()
549		for i := range r.ring {
550			r.ring[i] = slot{}
551		}
552		r.buf = 0
553		r.started = false
554		r.waiting = false
555		r.resyncReset()
556		r.rebaselines++
557	}
558	
559	// smallestBuffered scans the ring for the lowest occupied seq at or above next.
560	// All occupied seqs lie in [next, next+window), so this is bounded by window and
561	// runs only on the (rare) timeout path. Caller holds r.mu.
562	func (r *Resequencer) smallestBuffered() (uint64, bool) {
563		best := uint64(0)
564		found := false
565		for i := range r.ring {
566			c := &r.ring[i]
567			if c.occupied && c.seq >= r.next && (!found || c.seq < best) {
568				best = c.seq
569				found = true
570			}
571		}
572		return best, found
573	}
574	
575	// Buffered reports the number of frames currently held (not yet released). It is
576	// always <= window, which the bounded-memory tests assert.
577	func (r *Resequencer) Buffered() int {
578		r.mu.Lock()
579		defer r.mu.Unlock()
580		return r.buf
581	}
582	
583	// Pending reports the number of released-but-not-yet-popped items in the FIFO.
584	func (r *Resequencer) Pending() int {
585		r.mu.Lock()
586		defer r.mu.Unlock()
587		return len(r.ready) - r.readyPos
588	}
589	
590	// HighWater reports the maximum number of frames ever buffered simultaneously.
591	func (r *Resequencer) HighWater() int {
592		r.mu.Lock()
593		defer r.mu.Unlock()
594		return r.highWater
595	}
596	
597	// Stats is a snapshot of the resequencer's cumulative counters.
598	type Stats struct {
599		Released       uint64 // frames released for delivery
600		DroppedDup     uint64 // frames dropped as duplicates
601		DroppedOld     uint64 // frames dropped as already-past the release point
602		DroppedSuspect uint64 // out-of-band frames dropped while (not yet) corroborating
603		Skipped        uint64 // seqs skipped (lost) by window-advance or timeout
604		Resyncs        uint64 // release-point re-pins after a corroborated discontinuity
605		Rebaselines    uint64 // release-point re-baselines forced by a trusted control event (hub failover)
606	}
607	
608	// Stats returns a snapshot of the cumulative counters.
609	func (r *Resequencer) Stats() Stats {
610		r.mu.Lock()
611		defer r.mu.Unlock()
612		return Stats{
613			Released:       r.releasedN,
614			DroppedDup:     r.dropDup,
615			DroppedOld:     r.dropLate,
616			DroppedSuspect: r.dropSuspect,
617			Skipped:        r.skipped,
618			Resyncs:        r.resyncs,
619			Rebaselines:    r.rebaselines,
620		}
621	}
622

diff --git a/docs/design.md b/docs/design.md
index 4d4afdc..d5b55da 100644
--- a/docs/design.md
+++ b/docs/design.md
@@ -427,6 +427,45 @@ WireGuard anti-replay window sees the traffic — critical, because WG would
 otherwise drop legitimately-reordered datagrams. It runs its **own outer sequence
 space** and never touches the inner WireGuard counter (a core invariant).
 
+**Trusted re-baseline on a peer restart (T116/T119).** A DATA frame is
+unauthenticated, so the release point is normally moved only by the corroborating
+`resync` guard (several distinct low seqs within one window) — which a *single*
+low frame cannot trip. That is fine for reorder, but a **peer/concentrator process
+restart** resets the sender's outer-seq near 1 far below the release point the prior
+boot's high-rate stream advanced `next` to, and the restarted peer's wrapped WG init
+is a *lone* low frame — it would be dropped as *suspect* and the tunnel would never
+re-establish. The restart is detected on the **authenticated** liveness plane: the
+per-peer probe reflector reports an `epochChanged` when a probe adopts an
+already-adopted path under a **new session id** (a genuine restart, deduped once per
+epoch), and `dispatchInbound` re-baselines *that* peer's resequencer via
+`Resequencer.RebaselineToLow`. Because this fires on the demux-resolved per-peer view,
+the one call site covers both the edge single-concentrator primary and every
+concentrator per-peer resequencer, in either restart direction. Unlike the hub-failover
+`Rebaseline` (which unpins and re-anchors on the *next* frame), the **low-anchor**
+variant re-anchors only on a frame more than one window *below* the pre-rebaseline
+release point — so a stale HIGH-seq straggler still draining from the old boot's queues
+is suspect-dropped and cannot re-pin `next` high and block recovery (the D36 re-pin
+race). Two boundary rules keep the low-anchor gate from becoming a *blackhole*: (1) the
+gate is armed only when the release point is high enough for it to be satisfiable
+(`next > window+1`) — the restarted sender's first DATA is outer-seq ~1, so at a small
+anchor no low frame could ever satisfy `anchor - seq > window` and every frame would be
+suspect-dropped forever; at a small anchor (light traffic / an early restart / a
+crash-loop) it falls back to the plain unpin, which self-heals; and (2) a subsequent
+plain `Rebaseline` (a D32 hub failover) *clears* any still-pending low-anchor, so the
+fail-back stream is not re-classified against a now-stale anchor. Both re-baselines are
+sound because a hub switch and an authenticated epoch change are **trusted control
+events**, not forgeable wire frames. Two further rules keep the gate from blackholing
+under *loss* (D36's own premise): (3) the gate is **bounded** — the sole in-budget
+re-anchor frame at the tightest armed anchor (`window+2`) is outer-seq 1, and if that
+lone wrapped-init frame is *lost* every later new-boot frame fails `anchor - seq > window`
+and would suspect-drop forever, so after O(window) consecutive pending-low drops the gate
+falls back to the plain unpin and self-heals via the resync-corroboration path; and (4)
+FEC repair must not subvert the gate — `ObserveRecovered` normally bypasses `admit`, so a
+parity-recovered *old-boot* frame while the gate is armed is by definition pre-restart and
+is **dropped** (never seated), and the low-anchor re-anchor **clears the ring** (like
+`resync`) so no stale occupied cell survives to keep a head-of-line timeout live and jump
+`next` high past the restarted stream.
+
 ### FEC — `internal/fec` + `internal/adaptivefec`
 
 - `fec` implements Reed-Solomon over groups of *K* data shards + *M* parity

 		if pr.reflector != nil {
-			if echo, _, rerr := pr.reflector.Reflect(raw); rerr == nil {
+			if echo, epochChanged, rerr := pr.reflector.Reflect(raw); rerr == nil {

+**Trusted re-baseline on a peer restart (T116/T119).** A DATA frame is
+unauthenticated, so the release point is normally moved only by the corroborating
+`resync` guard (several distinct low seqs within one window) — which a *single*
+low frame cannot trip. That is fine for reorder, but a **peer/concentrator process
+restart** resets the sender's outer-seq near 1 far below the release point the prior
+boot's high-rate stream advanced `next` to, and the restarted peer's wrapped WG init
+is a *lone* low frame — it would be dropped as *suspect* and the tunnel would never
+re-establish. The restart is detected on the **authenticated** liveness plane: the
+per-peer probe reflector reports an `epochChanged` when a probe adopts an
+already-adopted path under a **new session id** (a genuine restart, deduped once per
+epoch), and `dispatchInbound` re-baselines *that* peer's resequencer via
+`Resequencer.RebaselineToLow`. Because this fires on the demux-resolved per-peer view,
+the one call site covers both the edge single-concentrator primary and every
+concentrator per-peer resequencer, in either restart direction. Unlike the hub-failover
+`Rebaseline` (which unpins and re-anchors on the *next* frame), the **low-anchor**
+variant re-anchors only on a frame more than one window *below* the pre-rebaseline
+release point — so a stale HIGH-seq straggler still draining from the old boot's queues
+is suspect-dropped and cannot re-pin `next` high and block recovery (the D36 re-pin
+race). Two boundary rules keep the low-anchor gate from becoming a *blackhole*: (1) the
+gate is armed only when the release point is high enough for it to be satisfiable
+(`next > window+1`) — the restarted sender's first DATA is outer-seq ~1, so at a small
+anchor no low frame could ever satisfy `anchor - seq > window` and every frame would be
+suspect-dropped forever; at a small anchor (light traffic / an early restart / a
+crash-loop) it falls back to the plain unpin, which self-heals; and (2) a subsequent
+plain `Rebaseline` (a D32 hub failover) *clears* any still-pending low-anchor, so the
+fail-back stream is not re-classified against a now-stale anchor. Both re-baselines are
+sound because a hub switch and an authenticated epoch change are **trusted control
+events**, not forgeable wire frames. Two further rules keep the gate from blackholing
+under *loss* (D36's own premise): (3) the gate is **bounded** — the sole in-budget
+re-anchor frame at the tightest armed anchor (`window+2`) is outer-seq 1, and if that
+lone wrapped-init frame is *lost* every later new-boot frame fails `anchor - seq > window`
+and would suspect-drop forever, so after O(window) consecutive pending-low drops the gate
+falls back to the plain unpin and self-heals via the resync-corroboration path; and (4)

+`next` high past the restarted stream.

diff --git a/internal/bind/peer_restart_rebaseline_test.go b/internal/bind/peer_restart_rebaseline_test.go
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..b217a42
--- /dev/null
+++ b/internal/bind/peer_restart_rebaseline_test.go
@@ -0,0 +1,227 @@
+package bind
+
+import (
+	"net"
+	"net/netip"
+	"testing"
+	"time"
+
+	"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/config"
+	"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/frame"
+)
+
+// The two sessionIDs a "restart" spans: the pre-restart boot and the post-restart boot.
+// Distinct values make the second adoption an epoch change (a new-sessionID adoption over
+// an already-adopted path), which is exactly what the reflector reports as epochChanged.
+const (
+	preRestartSession  uint64 = 0xAAAA0000AAAA0001
+	postRestartSession uint64 = 0xBBBB0000BBBB0002
+	// restartHighSeq is a release point far past resequencerWindow (2048): the pre-restart
+	// boot's busy stream advanced `next` here, so the restarted boot's low outer-seq init is
+	// >1 window below it — the SUSPECT region that, before T119, blackholed the wrapped init.
+	restartHighSeq uint64 = 9000
+)
+
+// deliverDATA encodes one DATA frame under psk and pushes it through the FULL receive path
+// (handleInbound → dispatchInbound's DATA branch → the peer's resequencer), exactly as a
+// wire datagram would arrive on view. It exercises the per-peer routing rather than poking
+// the resequencer directly, so the assertions read the resequencer the wiring actually feeds.
+func deliverDATA(t testing.TB, m *Multipath, view *peerPathState, psk config.Key, seq uint64, payload []byte, src netip.AddrPort) {
+	t.Helper()
+	codec, err := frame.NewCodec(psk)
+	if err != nil {
+		t.Fatalf("build data codec: %v", err)
+	}
+	raw, err := codec.Encode(nil, frame.Data{OuterSeq: seq, PathID: view.id, Payload: payload})
+	if err != nil {
+		t.Fatalf("encode data seq %d: %v", seq, err)
+	}
+	m.handleInbound(view, raw, src)
+}
+
+// reflectProbeIssuedChallenge drives ONE inbound PROBE (encoded under psk) through
+// handleInbound → dispatchInbound's non-echo Probe branch — the reflector adopt/restart
+// path plus the T119 epochChanged→RebaselineToLow wiring — and returns the issued challenge
+// carried in the reflected echo (read off the raw peer socket). Sending the reflector's own
+// live challenge back on the NEXT probe is what makes it adopt (the responder-contributed-
+// challenge protocol), so a caller learns the challenge here and echoes it next.
+func reflectProbeIssuedChallenge(t testing.TB, m *Multipath, view *peerPathState, psk config.Key, peer *net.UDPConn, peerAP netip.AddrPort, sessionID, probeSeq, echoedChallenge uint64) uint64 {
+	t.Helper()
+	raw, err := frame.Encode(psk, frame.Probe{
+		PathID:         view.id,
+		ProbeSeq:       probeSeq,
+		TimestampNanos: time.Now().UnixNano(),
+		SessionID:      sessionID,
+		Challenge:      echoedChallenge,
+	})
+	if err != nil {
+		t.Fatalf("encode probe (session %#x seq %d): %v", sessionID, probeSeq, err)
+	}
+	m.handleInbound(view, raw, peerAP)
+	codec, err := frame.NewCodec(psk)
+	if err != nil {
+		t.Fatalf("build echo codec: %v", err)
+	}
+	echo := readProbe(t, peer, codec)
+	return echo.Challenge
+}
+
+// runPeerRestartRebaselineScenario is the shared body of the T119 acceptance. It drives one
+// peer through: bootstrap adoption (no re-baseline), a busy stream advancing `next` far past
+// one window, an authenticated RESTART (new-sessionID adopt → epochChanged=true → the wiring
+// low-anchor re-baselines THIS peer's resequencer), a STALE-HIGH old-boot straggler that must
+// NOT re-pin `next` high, the wrapped LOW-seq init that must then admit, and a same-epoch probe
+// that must NOT re-anchor — all while a second bound peer (witnessPeer) is left untouched.
+//
+// It documents the failing-without-the-wiring contract: with the epochChanged→RebaselineToLow
+// call removed, the restart leaves `next` pinned high, Rebaselines stays 0, and the wrapped
+// low-seq init is SUSPECT-dropped — the delivery assertion below fails (the D36 repro).
+func runPeerRestartRebaselineScenario(
+	t *testing.T,
+	m *Multipath,
+	targetView *peerPathState, targetPeer *peerState, targetPSK config.Key,
+	witnessView *peerPathState, witnessPeer *peerState, witnessPSK config.Key,
+) {
+	t.Helper()
+	peer, peerAP := rawPeer(t)
+
+	rq := targetPeer.resequencer.Load()
+	wrq := witnessPeer.resequencer.Load()
+	if rq == nil || wrq == nil {
+		t.Fatalf("resequencer not instantiated: target=%v witness=%v", rq != nil, wrq != nil)
+	}
+
+	dataSrc := netip.MustParseAddrPort("203.0.113.9:51820")
+	lowSrc := netip.MustParseAddrPort("198.51.100.4:51820")
+
+	// --- Bootstrap adoption (session preRestartSession): learn challenge, then adopt. A
+	//     first-ever adoption is NOT a restart, so it must NOT re-baseline. ---
+	c := reflectProbeIssuedChallenge(t, m, targetView, targetPSK, peer, peerAP, preRestartSession, 0, 0)
+	_ = reflectProbeIssuedChallenge(t, m, targetView, targetPSK, peer, peerAP, preRestartSession, 1, c)
+	if got := rq.Stats().Rebaselines; got != 0 {
+		t.Fatalf("bootstrap adoption re-baselined the resequencer: Rebaselines=%d, want 0", got)
+	}
+
+	// --- The pre-restart boot's busy stream advances BOTH peers' release point far past one
+	//     window (via the real DATA receive path). ---
+	deliverDATA(t, m, targetView, targetPSK, restartHighSeq, []byte("boot-high"), dataSrc)
+	if _, ok := rq.Pop(); !ok {
+		t.Fatalf("high boot seq %d not delivered — release point did not advance", restartHighSeq)
+	}
+	deliverDATA(t, m, witnessView, witnessPSK, restartHighSeq, []byte("witness-high"), dataSrc)
+	if _, ok := wrq.Pop(); !ok {
+		t.Fatalf("witness high boot seq not delivered — its release point did not advance")
+	}
+
+	// --- Authenticated PEER RESTART (session postRestartSession): learn the live challenge,
+	//     then adopt with it. This adoption is over an ALREADY-adopted path under a DIFFERENT
+	//     session → epochChanged=true → the T119 wiring low-anchor re-baselines THIS peer. ---
+	c2 := reflectProbeIssuedChallenge(t, m, targetView, targetPSK, peer, peerAP, postRestartSession, 0, 0)
+	_ = reflectProbeIssuedChallenge(t, m, targetView, targetPSK, peer, peerAP, postRestartSession, 1, c2)
+	if got := rq.Stats().Rebaselines; got != 1 {
+		t.Fatalf("peer restart did NOT re-baseline the target resequencer: Rebaselines=%d, want 1 "+
+			"(the epochChanged→RebaselineToLow wiring is the code under test)", got)
+	}
+
+	// --- STALE-HIGH RACE: a stale OLD-boot high-seq straggler still draining from carrier/modem
+	//     queues lands BEFORE the low init. It must be SUSPECT-dropped and must NOT re-pin `next`
+	//     high (which a plain unpin-and-trust-next re-baseline would allow, blocking recovery). ---
+	beforeSuspect := rq.Stats().DroppedSuspect
+	deliverDATA(t, m, targetView, targetPSK, restartHighSeq+50, []byte("stale-high-straggler"), dataSrc)
+	if it, ok := rq.Pop(); ok {
+		t.Fatalf("stale-high straggler was DELIVERED (%q) — it re-pinned next high (D36 race not closed)", it.Payload)
+	}
+	if rq.Stats().DroppedSuspect <= beforeSuspect {
+		t.Fatalf("stale-high straggler not SUSPECT-dropped: DroppedSuspect stayed %d", beforeSuspect)
+	}
+
+	// --- The wrapped WG init (restarted stream, outer-seq ~1) now admits: it is >1 window below
+	//     the pre-rebaseline release point, so it re-anchors and DELIVERS, and it must NOT itself
+	//     count as a suspect drop. ---
+	suspectBeforeLow := rq.Stats().DroppedSuspect
+	deliverDATA(t, m, targetView, targetPSK, 1, []byte("wrapped-wg-init"), lowSrc)
+	it, ok := rq.Pop()
+	if !ok || string(it.Payload) != "wrapped-wg-init" {
+		t.Fatalf("wrapped low-seq init NOT delivered after restart re-anchor: ok=%v payload=%q", ok, it.Payload)
+	}
+	if got := rq.Stats().DroppedSuspect; got != suspectBeforeLow {
+		t.Fatalf("the low-seq init was counted as a suspect drop: DroppedSuspect %d → %d", suspectBeforeLow, got)
+	}
+
+	// --- A SAME-epoch (non-restart) probe must NOT re-anchor: a within-session probe reports
+	//     epochChanged=false, so the wiring leaves the release point alone. ---
+	rebBefore := rq.Stats().Rebaselines
+	_ = reflectProbeIssuedChallenge(t, m, targetView, targetPSK, peer, peerAP, postRestartSession, 2, 0)
+	if got := rq.Stats().Rebaselines; got != rebBefore {
+		t.Fatalf("a same-epoch (non-restart) probe re-baselined: Rebaselines %d → %d", rebBefore, got)
+	}
+
+	// --- The other bound peer's resequencer is UNDISTURBED by the target's restart. ---
+	if got := wrq.Stats().Rebaselines; got != 0 {
+		t.Fatalf("witness peer re-baselined by the target's restart: Rebaselines=%d, want 0", got)
+	}
+	wSuspectBefore := wrq.Stats().DroppedSuspect
+	deliverDATA(t, m, witnessView, witnessPSK, 1, []byte("witness-should-drop"), lowSrc)
+	if it, ok := wrq.Pop(); ok {
+		t.Fatalf("witness delivered a low seq (%q) — its release point was disturbed by the target's restart", it.Payload)
+	}
+	if got := wrq.Stats().DroppedSuspect; got <= wSuspectBefore {
+		t.Fatalf("witness did not reject the low seq as suspect (its next moved): DroppedSuspect stayed %d", wSuspectBefore)
+	}
+}
+
+// TestPeerRestartRebaselinesPrimaryResequencer restarts the PRIMARY (edge single-concentrator)
+// peer and asserts the wrapped low-seq init re-anchors while a second bound peer is untouched.
+func TestPeerRestartRebaselinesPrimaryResequencer(t *testing.T) {
+	pskA := testKey(t, 0x11)
+	pskB := testKey(t, 0x22)
+	m, beta := newConcentratorPairForRestart(t, pskA, pskB)
+
+	primary := m.peerState
+	runPeerRestartRebaselineScenario(t, m,
+		m.paths[0], primary, pskA,
+		peerPathByName(beta, "a"), beta, pskB)
+}
+
+// TestPeerRestartRebaselinesConcentratorPeerResequencer restarts an AddConcentratorPeer peer
+// and asserts the SAME single wiring site re-anchors its per-peer resequencer, leaving the
+// primary untouched — proving the demux-resolved per-peer view covers concentrator peers too.
+func TestPeerRestartRebaselinesConcentratorPeerResequencer(t *testing.T) {
+	pskA := testKey(t, 0x33)
+	pskB := testKey(t, 0x44)
+	m, beta := newConcentratorPairForRestart(t, pskA, pskB)
+
+	primary := m.peerState
+	runPeerRestartRebaselineScenario(t, m,
+		peerPathByName(beta, "a"), beta, pskB,
+		m.paths[0], primary, pskA)
+}
+
+// newConcentratorPairForRestart builds an Open 2-peer concentrator over one shared socket: the
+// primary keyed on pskA and a beta peer registered via AddConcentratorPeer keyed on pskB, with
+// beta's heavy receive datapath (its resequencer) instantiated so a test can drive DATA at it
+// directly rather than waiting for the demux to lazily bind its first PROBE.
+func newConcentratorPairForRestart(t *testing.T, pskA, pskB config.Key) (*Multipath, *peerState) {
+	t.Helper()
+	clk := newFakeClock()
+	paths := loopbackPaths(1) // one shared socket, path "a"
+	m, _, _ := newProbingMultipath(t, paths, pskA, clk)
+
+	betaSched, betaProbers, betaFactory := concPeerWiring(t, paths, pskB, 0x0BEEF, clk)
+	if err := m.AddConcentratorPeer("beta", pskB, betaSched, betaProbers, betaFactory); err != nil {
+		t.Fatalf("AddConcentratorPeer: %v", err)
+	}
+	if _, _, err := m.Open(0); err != nil {
+		t.Fatalf("Open: %v", err)
+	}
+	t.Cleanup(func() { _ = m.Close() })
+
+	beta := m.peersByName["beta"]
+	if beta == nil {
+		t.Fatal("beta peer not registered")
+	}
+	// A concentrator peer's resequencer is lazily built on its first bound PROBE; instantiate it
+	// eagerly here so the test drives its receive plane deterministically.
+	m.ensurePeerReceiveInstantiated(beta)
+	return m, beta
+}
diff --git a/internal/reseq/reseq_test.go b/internal/reseq/reseq_test.go

--- a/internal/reseq/reseq_test.go
+++ b/internal/reseq/reseq_test.go
@@ -798,3 +798,339 @@ func TestRebaselineDiscardsBufferedPreSwitchFrames(t *testing.T) {

+// TestRebaselineToLowSurvivesStaleHighStragglerRace is the reseq-level D36 repro (plan
+// review R126). Under the saturation precondition the plain Rebaseline (unpin + trust
+// the NEXT frame) loses a race: after a PEER RESTART re-baseline, a stale HIGH-seq

+// and re-pin `next` HIGH, and with once-per-epoch restart dedup recovery is then blocked.
+// RebaselineToLow re-anchors ONLY on a frame more than one window below the pre-rebaseline
+// release point, so the stale-high straggler is SUSPECT-dropped and the subsequent low
+// init still admits. The parallel with plain Rebaseline is deliberate: swap RebaselineToLow
+// for Rebaseline here and the straggler re-pins next high, the low init is dropped, and the
+// final delivery assertion fails.
+func TestRebaselineToLowSurvivesStaleHighStragglerRace(t *testing.T) {
+	clk := newFakeClock()
+	const window = 64
+	r := reseq.New(window, time.Second, clk)
+

+	// window (next == priorHi afterwards).
+	const priorHi = 200
+	for s := uint64(0); s < priorHi; s++ {
+		r.Observe(s, payloadOf(s), testSrc)

+	// the current (high) release point rather than unpinning outright.

+		t.Fatalf("Rebaselines = %d, want 1", s.Rebaselines)

+	// SUSPECT-dropped and must NOT re-pin `next` high (which a plain Rebaseline would

+	const staleHigh = priorHi + 5 // above the old release point: a genuine "high" straggler
+	beforeSuspect := r.Stats().DroppedSuspect
+	r.RebaselineToLow() // idempotent: a repeated restart signal keeps the original high anchor
+	if s := r.Stats(); s.Rebaselines != 2 {
+		t.Fatalf("Rebaselines = %d after a repeated restart signal, want 2", s.Rebaselines)
+	}
+	r.Observe(staleHigh, payloadOf(staleHigh), testSrc)
+	if got := drain(r); len(got) != 0 {
+		t.Fatalf("stale-high straggler was DELIVERED %v — it re-pinned next high (D36 race not closed)", got)
+	}
+	if r.Stats().DroppedSuspect <= beforeSuspect {
+		t.Fatal("stale-high straggler was not SUSPECT-dropped (DroppedSuspect did not increase)")

+// TestRebaselineToLowNoOpBeforeFirstObserve pins that a low-anchor re-baseline on an
+// UNSTARTED ring (a torn-down peer re-instantiated a fresh resequencer) enters NO pending
+// mode: the first Observe pins `next` normally, whatever its seq, exactly as a fresh ring
+// behaves. It bumps the diagnostic counter but must not blackhole the first stream.
+func TestRebaselineToLowNoOpBeforeFirstObserve(t *testing.T) {
+	clk := newFakeClock()
+	r := reseq.New(64, time.Second, clk)
+
+	r.RebaselineToLow()
+	if s := r.Stats(); s.Rebaselines != 1 {
+		t.Fatalf("Rebaselines = %d, want 1", s.Rebaselines)
+	}
+	// A fresh high-seq stream pins next and delivers in order — no pending-low gate.
+	r.Observe(5000, payloadOf(5000), testSrc)
+	r.Observe(5001, payloadOf(5001), testSrc)
+	if got := drain(r); !equalSeqs(got, []uint64{5000, 5001}) {
+		t.Fatalf("unstarted RebaselineToLow blackholed the first stream: delivered %v, want [5000 5001]", got)
+	}
+}
+
+// TestRebaselineToLowSmallAnchorSelfHeals is the FIX-1 regression (round-2 review). A
+// RebaselineToLow at a SMALL release point (next <= window) must NOT arm the low-anchor
+// gate: the re-anchor predicate `seq < anchor && anchor - seq > window` is UNSATISFIABLE
+// for the restarted sender's first DATA (outer-seq ~1) when anchor <= window+1, so a
+// pin would blackhole the whole restarted stream FOREVER — a regression from the pre-T119
+// resync self-heal. Realistic trigger: light traffic / an early restart / a crash-loop
+// (a first restart re-anchors next~1, a second restart within one window pins a tiny
+// anchor). The fix falls back to a plain unpin at a small anchor, so the new-boot stream
+// re-anchors on its first frame and DELIVERS.
+func TestRebaselineToLowSmallAnchorSelfHeals(t *testing.T) {
+	clk := newFakeClock()
+	const window = 64
+	r := reseq.New(window, time.Second, clk)
+
+	// Old boot: only 50 contiguous frames, so the release point is SMALL (next == 50 <=
+	// window). This is the light-traffic / early-restart precondition.
+	const oldBoot = 50
+	for s := uint64(0); s < oldBoot; s++ {
+		r.Observe(s, payloadOf(s), testSrc)
+	}
+	_ = drain(r)
+
+	// Authenticated peer restart at the small anchor. FIX 1: no pending-low gate armed
+	// (it would be unsatisfiable); plain unpin instead.
+	r.RebaselineToLow()
+	if s := r.Stats(); s.Rebaselines != 1 {
+		t.Fatalf("Rebaselines = %d, want 1", s.Rebaselines)
+	}
+
+	// The restarted new-boot stream (outer-seq restarts near 1). Before the fix these were
+	// all SUSPECT-dropped forever (a permanent blackhole); after the fix the first frame
+	// re-anchors next and the stream DELIVERS in order.
+	var want []uint64
+	for s := uint64(1); s <= 8; s++ {
+		r.Observe(s, payloadOf(s), testSrc)
+		want = append(want, s)
+	}
+	if got := drain(r); !equalSeqs(got, want) {
+		t.Fatalf("small-anchor restart blackholed the new-boot stream: delivered %v, want %v", got, want)
+	}
+	// The stream self-healed on the FIRST frame, not by dropping the whole window.
+	if s := r.Stats(); s.DroppedSuspect != 0 {
+		t.Fatalf("small-anchor restart SUSPECT-dropped %d new-boot frames; want 0 (plain unpin re-anchors)", s.DroppedSuspect)
+	}
+}
+
+// TestRebaselineToLowThenRebaselineDelivers is the FIX-2 regression (round-2 review). A
+// plain Rebaseline() (the D32 hub-failover path) must CLEAR a pending low-anchor left by a
+// prior RebaselineToLow whose restarted low init has not yet re-anchored next. Without the
+// fix, Rebaseline resets `started` but leaves pendingLow armed against the stale
+// pendingLowAnchor, so every post-failover fail-back frame is re-classified against that
+// stale anchor and SUSPECT-dropped — violating Rebaseline's "next Observe re-anchors next"
+// postcondition. The fix clears pendingLow, restoring the plain unpin-and-trust-next path.
+func TestRebaselineToLowThenRebaselineDelivers(t *testing.T) {
+	clk := newFakeClock()
+	const window = 64
+	r := reseq.New(window, time.Second, clk)
+
+	// Prior boot advances the release point well past one window: next == 200.
+	const priorHi = 200
+	for s := uint64(0); s < priorHi; s++ {
+		r.Observe(s, payloadOf(s), testSrc)
+	}
+	_ = drain(r)
+
+	// Peer restart arms the pending low-anchor at 200 (the low init has NOT yet arrived).
+	r.RebaselineToLow()
+
+	// A D32 hub failover now Rebaselines the SAME resequencer. FIX 2: this clears the
+	// pending low-anchor so the standby's fail-back stream is not gated against the stale
+	// anchor.
+	r.Rebaseline()
+
+	// The standby's fail-back stream (a fresh outer-seq run, here 300..399). Before the fix
+	// these were all SUSPECT-dropped (0/100 delivered) against the stale pendingLowAnchor=200;
+	// after the fix the first frame re-anchors next and the stream DELIVERS in order.
+	var want []uint64
+	for s := uint64(300); s < 400; s++ {
+		r.Observe(s, payloadOf(s), testSrc)
+		want = append(want, s)
+	}
+	if got := drain(r); !equalSeqs(got, want) {
+		t.Fatalf("RebaselineToLow→Rebaseline left the pending gate in force: delivered %d frames, want %d", len(got), len(want))
+	}
+	if s := r.Stats(); s.DroppedSuspect != 0 {
+		t.Fatalf("post-Rebaseline fail-back stream SUSPECT-dropped %d frames; want 0 (stale pendingLow not cleared)", s.DroppedSuspect)
+	}
+}
+
+// isContiguousAscending reports whether s is a strictly +1 ascending run.
+func isContiguousAscending(s []uint64) bool {
+	for i := 1; i < len(s); i++ {
+		if s[i] != s[i-1]+1 {
+			return false
+		}
+	}
+	return true
+}
+
+// TestRebaselineToLowPlainUnpinAtWindowPlusOne is the round-3 FIX-3 lower boundary
+// (review R150). At release point next == window+1 the low-anchor gate is NOT armed:
+// the re-anchor predicate `seq < anchor && anchor - seq > window` requires
+// anchor >= window+2 to admit any positive seq, so at anchor == window+1 it is
+// unsatisfiable and arming would blackhole. RebaselineToLow must fall back to a PLAIN
+// unpin here, so the restarted stream re-anchors on its first frame with no suspect
+// drops — exactly the D32 hub-failover behaviour.
+func TestRebaselineToLowPlainUnpinAtWindowPlusOne(t *testing.T) {
+	clk := newFakeClock()
+	const window = 64
+	r := reseq.New(window, time.Second, clk)
+
+	// Advance the release point to EXACTLY window+1 (observe seqs 0..window, i.e.
+	// window+1 frames, leaves next == window+1).
+	for s := uint64(0); s <= window; s++ {
+		r.Observe(s, payloadOf(s), testSrc)
+	}
+	_ = drain(r) // next == window+1
+
+	r.RebaselineToLow()
+
+	// Restarted new-boot stream (outer-seq near 1): the plain unpin re-anchors on the
+	// first frame and the stream DELIVERS with zero suspect drops (gate never armed).
+	var want []uint64
+	for s := uint64(1); s <= 6; s++ {
+		r.Observe(s, payloadOf(s), testSrc)
+		want = append(want, s)
+	}
+	if got := drain(r); !equalSeqs(got, want) {
+		t.Fatalf("window+1 anchor did not take the plain-unpin path: delivered %v, want %v", got, want)
+	}
+	if s := r.Stats(); s.DroppedSuspect != 0 {
+		t.Fatalf("window+1 anchor armed the gate (SUSPECT-dropped %d); want plain unpin, 0 drops", s.DroppedSuspect)
+	}
+}
+
+// TestRebaselineToLowArmedAtWindowPlusTwoSatisfiedBySeq1 is the round-3 FIX-3 upper
+// boundary (review R150). At release point next == window+2 the gate IS armed and its
+// SOLE in-budget re-anchor frame is seq 1 (anchor - 1 == window+1 > window). A stale
+// HIGH straggler is SUSPECT-dropped; seq 1 then re-anchors and the new boot delivers.
+// This pins the exact boundary the round-2 guard (arm only when next > window+1) opens.
+func TestRebaselineToLowArmedAtWindowPlusTwoSatisfiedBySeq1(t *testing.T) {
+	clk := newFakeClock()
+	const window = 64
+	r := reseq.New(window, time.Second, clk)
+
+	// Advance to EXACTLY window+2 (observe seqs 0..window+1 == window+2 frames).
+	for s := uint64(0); s <= window+1; s++ {
+		r.Observe(s, payloadOf(s), testSrc)
+	}
+	_ = drain(r) // next == window+2
+
+	r.RebaselineToLow() // arms pendingLow at anchor == window+2
+
+	// A stale-high old-boot straggler must be SUSPECT-dropped, not re-pin next high.
+	const staleHigh = window + 5
+	r.Observe(staleHigh, payloadOf(staleHigh), testSrc)
+	if got := drain(r); len(got) != 0 {
+		t.Fatalf("stale-high straggler delivered %v at the window+2 boundary — gate not armed", got)
+	}
+
+	// seq 1 — the ONLY in-budget re-anchor at this boundary — re-anchors and delivers.
+	r.Observe(1, payloadOf(1), testSrc)
+	r.Observe(2, payloadOf(2), testSrc)
+	if got := drain(r); !equalSeqs(got, []uint64{1, 2}) {
+		t.Fatalf("seq 1 did not re-anchor the window+2 gate: delivered %v, want [1 2]", got)
+	}
+}
+
+// TestRebaselineToLowGateRecoversWhenInitFrameLost is the round-3 FIX-3 core regression
+// (fable probe, reproduced 0/499; review R150). At anchor == window+2 the only in-budget
+// re-anchor frame is seq 1. If that lone wrapped-init frame is LOST — loss under
+// saturation is the D36 premise — every later new-boot frame fails `anchor - seq > window`
+// and, on the round-2 logic, is SUSPECT-dropped FOREVER (a permanent blackhole: 0/N
+// delivered). The bounded gate falls back to a plain unpin after O(window) drops, so the
+// new-boot stream self-heals and DELIVERS its tail instead of blackholing.
+func TestRebaselineToLowGateRecoversWhenInitFrameLost(t *testing.T) {
+	clk := newFakeClock()
+	const window = 64
+	r := reseq.New(window, time.Second, clk)
+
+	// Advance to EXACTLY window+2 so the gate arms with seq 1 as the sole re-anchor.
+	for s := uint64(0); s <= window+1; s++ {
+		r.Observe(s, payloadOf(s), testSrc)
+	}
+	_ = drain(r) // next == window+2
+	r.RebaselineToLow()
+
+	// seq 1 is LOST. The restarted stream continues at 2,3,...,last. Each fails the
+	// re-anchor predicate and is SUSPECT-dropped until the bounded gate falls back.
+	const last = 3 * window
+	for s := uint64(2); s <= last; s++ {
+		r.Observe(s, payloadOf(s), testSrc)
+	}
+	got := drain(r)
+
+	if len(got) == 0 {
+		t.Fatal("init frame lost blackholed the whole new-boot stream (0/N) — bounded gate did not fall back")
+	}
+	if !isContiguousAscending(got) {
+		t.Fatalf("recovered delivery is not a contiguous run: %v", got)
+	}
+	if got[len(got)-1] != last {
+		t.Fatalf("recovered stream did not catch up: last delivered %d, want %d", got[len(got)-1], last)
+	}
+	if got[0] <= 1 {
+		t.Fatalf("stream delivered from seq %d — the bounded gate should drop a run before falling back", got[0])
+	}
+	if s := r.Stats(); s.DroppedSuspect == 0 {
+		t.Fatal("expected the bounded gate to SUSPECT-drop a bounded run before self-healing; got 0")
+	}
+}
+
+// TestObserveRecoveredDroppedWhileGateArmed is the round-3 FIX-4 regression (fable probe,
+// reproduced next 2→210 Skipped:208; review R150). FEC ObserveRecovered is production-wired
+// to the SAME per-peer resequencer and otherwise bypasses the low-anchor gate, so a parity-
+// recovered OLD-boot frame in [anchor, anchor+window) would be PLACED while the gate is
+// armed; then the re-anchor (which did not clear the ring) leaves that stale cell live, and
+// expire() jumps next HIGH past the restarted stream, delivering a stale frame. The fix (a)
+// clears the ring on re-anchor and (b) DROPS recovered frames while the gate is armed. This
+// pins both: a recovered frame while armed is dropped and seats nothing, and the subsequent
+// low init re-anchors cleanly with no stale delivery and no high re-pin.
+func TestObserveRecoveredDroppedWhileGateArmed(t *testing.T) {
+	clk := newFakeClock()
+	const window = 64
+	r := reseq.New(window, time.Second, clk)
+
+	// Busy prior boot: next == 200, well past one window.
+	const priorHi = 200
+	for s := uint64(0); s < priorHi; s++ {
+		r.Observe(s, payloadOf(s), testSrc)
+	}
+	_ = drain(r)
+	r.RebaselineToLow() // arms pendingLow at 200
+
+	// A parity-recovered OLD-boot frame inside [anchor, anchor+window) arrives while armed.
+	// It must be DROPPED (not placed) and must seat NOTHING in the ring.
+	const staleRecovered = priorHi + 5
+	if placed := r.ObserveRecovered(staleRecovered, payloadOf(staleRecovered), testSrc); placed {
+		t.Fatal("recovered old-boot frame was PLACED while the low-anchor gate was armed")
+	}
+	if b := r.Buffered(); b != 0 {
+		t.Fatalf("recovered-while-armed seated %d cells; want 0 (gate must drop it)", b)
+	}
+
+	// The genuine restarted low init now re-anchors. Delivery must be the NEW-boot stream
+	// from seq 1 — never the stale recovered frame, and next must NOT have jumped high.
+	r.Observe(1, payloadOf(1), testSrc)
+	r.Observe(2, payloadOf(2), testSrc)
+	got := drain(r)
+	if !equalSeqs(got, []uint64{1, 2}) {
+		t.Fatalf("after a recovered-while-armed interleave the restart delivered %v, want [1 2] (stale re-pin/deliver)", got)
+	}
+}

+package bind
+
+import (
+	"net"
+	"net/netip"
+	"testing"
+	"time"

+	"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/config"
